title: Kinematography and the Limits of Writing-System Typology
subtitle: Signed writing as a pressure test for grapholinguistics
summary: Native capstone paper for the Writing and Representation in Sutton SignWriting series on signed writing, kinematography, and the limits of current writing-system typology.
synopsis: This entry develops kinematography as a candidate analytic term for writing whose primary representational work is the durable inscription of articulatory-spatial-kinetic form. It distinguishes kinematography from phonography, morphography, featural description, pictography, notation, semasiography, and plane-based organization, while adding criteria, failure conditions, and a worked typological contrast that keep the term bounded as a pressure test rather than settled field terminology.

slides.title: Why Another Term
slides.summary: Kinematography is proposed as a bounded pressure-test term for writing whose primary representational work is articulatory, spatial, and kinetic.

image_prompts.concrete: Grapholinguistics slide showing phonography, morphography, pictography, notation, and kinematography as candidate terms around signed writing
image_prompts.abstract: A new typological term tested against existing categories

slides.speech: This paper asks whether another term is useful. Kinematography is proposed as a bounded pressure-test term for writing whose primary representational work is articulatory, spatial, and kinetic.

slides.title: What It Names
slides.summary: The term names a representational axis, not the same thing as plane-based organization.

image_prompts.concrete: Two-axis slide distinguishing plane-based organization from kinematographic representation in SignWriting
image_prompts.abstract: Two separate axes locating signed writing more precisely

slides.speech: The paper keeps two axes separate. Plane-based writing describes how the written unit is organized. Kinematography would name the representational target: durable inscription of articulatory, spatial, and kinetic form.

slides.title: Bounded Tests
slides.summary: The paper defines minimum criteria and failure conditions so the term does not become an all-purpose label.

image_prompts.concrete: Criteria slide showing minimum test and failure conditions for the term kinematography
image_prompts.abstract: A candidate term passing through a careful test gate

bullets: Must not be mere pictorial depiction
Must remain distinct from notation and encoding
slides.speech: The term only helps if it is bounded. The paper gives criteria and failure conditions so kinematography does not become a flattering synonym for anything visual or signed.

slides.title: Typology Pressure Test
slides.summary: The value of the term is that it reveals where current grapholinguistic categories need sharper treatment of signed writing.

image_prompts.concrete: Critical typology slide showing signed writing pressing against existing grapholinguistic categories and opening a refinement path
image_prompts.abstract: A pressure point in a typology grid revealing a missing category

bullets: Connects to the Critical Review series
slides.speech: The point is not to declare final terminology. The point is to reveal where current grapholinguistic categories strain and to give future work a sharper way to ask what signed writing represents.

slides.title: What Would Count Against It
slides.summary: The term should be rejected or revised if it collapses into pictography, notation, general visuality, or claims not supported by written practice.

image_prompts.concrete: Pressure-test slide showing kinematography passing or failing criteria, with failure paths labeled pictography, notation, general visuality, and unsupported terminology
image_prompts.abstract: A candidate term tested by clear failure conditions

bullets: Reject it if it becomes too broad
Reject it if it only means pictorial
Reject it if notation explains the case better
Revise it if practice does not support it
slides.speech: A pressure-test term needs failure conditions. Kinematography should be rejected or revised if it becomes a vague synonym for visuality, collapses into pictography, is better explained as notation, or lacks support from actual written practice. That discipline is what makes the term worth testing.
slides.notes: This slide keeps the candidate term provisional and scholarly. It should reduce the risk that readers hear it as branding.
